content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus Chapter 23

**Chapter:** Leviticus 23 — The Sacred Calendar (44 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Sources:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Total Disagreements:** 52

---

## Classification Key

- **[WORD]** — Different word choice for same Hebrew term
- **[STRUCT]** — Different sentence structure or clause arrangement
- **[INTERP]** — Interpretive difference (one agent reads the Hebrew differently)
- **[ADD]** — One agent adds words or ideas not in the Hebrew
- **[OMIT]** — One agent omits a word or element present in the Hebrew
- **[NAME]** — Proper name or title form

---

## Disagreements by Verse

**V. 1 — Moses / Moshe** [NAME]
- EMET: "And the LORD spoke to **Moshe**, saying"
- LOGOS: "And the LORD spoke to **Moses**, saying"
- PNEUMA: "The LORD spoke to **Moses**"
- **Ruling:** "Moses" — English name form per project convention.
- *Applies also at vv. 9, 23, 26, 33, and 44.*

**V. 1 — Conjunctive "And"** [STRUCT]
- EMET: "**And** the LORD spoke"
- LOGOS: "**And** the LORD spoke"
- PNEUMA: (omits opening "And")
- **Ruling:** "And the LORD spoke" — the *vav*-consecutive opening is preserved. PNEUMA drops the conjunction throughout multiple divine-speech introductions.
- *Applies at vv. 9, 23, 26, and 33.*

**V. 2 — "Sons of Israel" / "Israelites"** [WORD]
- EMET: "sons of **Yisra'el**"
- LOGOS: "sons of **Israel**"
- PNEUMA: "**the Israelites**"
- **Ruling:** "sons of Israel" — project term for *bney yisrael*. PNEUMA drops "sons of" and uses the collective noun. Applies throughout chapter (vv. 2, 10, 24, 34, 42, 43, 44).

**V. 2 — "Holy convocations" / "sacred assemblies"** [WORD]
- EMET: "**holy convocations** [*miqra'ei qodesh*]"
- LOGOS: "**holy convocations**"
- PNEUMA: "**sacred assemblies**"
- **Ruling:** "holy convocations" — project ruling. *Miqra qodesh* is a technical term; "sacred assemblies" (PNEUMA) is an acceptable rendering but is not the project standard. PNEUMA uses "sacred assemblies" consistently throughout the chapter in place of "holy convocations."
- *Applies at every occurrence: vv. 2, 3, 4, 7, 8, 21, 24, 25, 27, 35, 36.*

**V. 3 — Work prohibition: "all work" vs. "no work" vs. "regular work"** [WORD/INTERP]
- EMET: "all labor you shall not do [*kol-melakhah*]"
- LOGOS: "You shall do no **work**"
- PNEUMA: "You must not do any **regular work** on it"
- **Ruling:** "You shall do no work" (LOGOS) for v. 3. The MT uses *kol-melakhah* — the absolute prohibition — not the lesser *melaket avodah*. PNEUMA's "regular work" imports the festival-day formula into the Sabbath, obscuring the distinction.

**V. 3 — "Sabbath of solemn rest" / "sabbath of complete rest"** [WORD]
- EMET: "a sabbath of **complete rest** [*shabbat shabbaton*]"
- LOGOS: "a sabbath of **solemn rest**"
- PNEUMA: "a Sabbath of **complete rest**"
- **Ruling:** "sabbath of solemn rest" — LOGOS/ESV convention adopted for consensus.

**V. 3 — "In all your dwelling places" / "wherever you live"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "in all your **dwellings**"
- LOGOS: "in all your **dwelling places**"
- PNEUMA: "**wherever you live**"
- **Ruling:** "in all your dwelling places" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the legal formula *be-khol moshvoteikhem*.
- *Applies at vv. 14, 17, 21, 31, 41.*

**V. 5 — "At twilight" / "between the two evenings"** [WORD/INTERP]
- EMET: "**between the two evenings** [*bein ha-arbayim*]"
- LOGOS: "**at twilight**"
- PNEUMA: "**at twilight**"
- **Ruling:** "at twilight" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "between the two evenings" is the most literal rendering of *bein ha-arbayim* and preserves the interpretive ambiguity regarding the precise time of the Passover slaughter (debated between Pharisees, Samaritans, and Karaites). The consensus adopts "at twilight" as the standard formal equivalent, but EMET's "between the two evenings" has genuine translational merit in this context.

**V. 7 — "Laborious work" / "ordinary work" / "regular work"** [WORD]
- EMET: "all **laborious work** [*melaket avodah*]"
- LOGOS: "no **ordinary work**"
- PNEUMA: "no **regular work**"
- **Ruling:** "laborious work" — EMET's rendering of the technical compound *melaket avodah* is preferred for the consensus. This is a significant disagreement that recurs at every festival day prohibition (vv. 7, 8, 21, 25, 35, 36). LOGOS's "ordinary work" and PNEUMA's "regular work" are both defensible but lose the intensifying force of the Hebrew compound.

**V. 8 — "Fire-offering" / "food offering"** [WORD/INTERP]
- EMET: "a **fire-offering** [*isheh*] to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "a **food offering** to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "a **food offering** to the LORD"
- **Ruling:** "food offering" — LOGOS/PNEUMA, following ESV/NASB convention. EMET's "fire-offering" reflects the traditional etymology from *esh* ("fire"). The lexical question of whether *isheh* derives from *esh* ("fire") or a Ugaritic root meaning "food/provision" is unresolved; the consensus adopts the modern convention. This disagreement recurs at every occurrence of *isheh la-YHWH* throughout the chapter (vv. 8, 13, 18, 25, 27, 36, 37).

**V. 10 — "Come into" / "enter"** [WORD]
- EMET: "when you **come into** the land"
- LOGOS: "when you **come into** the land"
- PNEUMA: "when you **enter** the land"
- **Ruling:** "come into" — LOGOS/EMET. Substantively equivalent; LOGOS is preferred.

**V. 10 — "Reap its harvest" / "harvest its grain"** [WORD]
- EMET: "you **harvest its harvest** [*qtsartem et-qetsira*]"
- LOGOS: "you **reap its harvest**"
- PNEUMA: "you **harvest its grain**"
- **Ruling:** "you reap its harvest" — LOGOS. The cognate Hebrew construction (*qtsartem et-qetsira* — "you harvest its harvest") is best rendered with the root-consistent "reap its harvest." PNEUMA's "grain" specifies the produce unnecessarily.

**V. 11 — "So that you may be accepted" / "so it will be accepted"** [INTERP/STRUCT]
- EMET: "**for your acceptance** [*li-rtsoneikhem*]"
- LOGOS: "**so that you may be accepted**"
- PNEUMA: "**so it will be accepted on your behalf**"
- **Ruling:** "so that you may be accepted" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "it will be accepted" makes the offering the subject of acceptance, whereas the Hebrew *li-rtsoneikhem* indicates the people's acceptance before YHWH.

**V. 12 — "Shall offer" / "shall prepare"** [WORD]
- EMET: "you shall **prepare** [*ta'asu*] a male lamb"
- LOGOS: "you shall **offer** a male lamb"
- PNEUMA: "you must **sacrifice** a burnt offering"
- **Ruling:** "you shall offer" — LOGOS. *Ta'asu* is literally "do/make," but "offer" is the idiomatic English equivalent in a sacrificial context. EMET's "prepare" is too weak; PNEUMA's "sacrifice" adds a specific action beyond the Hebrew.

**V. 13 — "Fine flour" / "finest flour"** [WORD]
- EMET: "**fine flour** [*solet*]"
- LOGOS: "**fine flour**"
- PNEUMA: "**the finest flour**"
- **Ruling:** "fine flour" — LOGOS/EMET. *Solet* = sifted/refined wheat flour; "finest" (PNEUMA) adds a superlative not in the Hebrew.

**V. 14 — "Until this very day" / "until this same day"** [WORD]
- EMET: "until **this very day** [*ad etsem ha-yom ha-zeh*]"
- LOGOS: "until **this same day**"
- PNEUMA: "until **the very day**"
- **Ruling:** "until this very day" — EMET. The *etsem ha-yom* formula ("the bone/substance of the day") is the Hebrew emphatic idiom for "this very day"; "this same day" (LOGOS) softens the emphasis.

**V. 15 — "For yourselves" retained / dropped** [OMIT]
- EMET: "you shall count **for yourselves** [*u-sefartem lakhem*]"
- LOGOS: "you shall count" (drops *lakhem*)
- PNEUMA: "Count off" (drops *lakhem*)
- **Ruling:** "you shall count for yourselves" — EMET. The *lakhem* ("for yourselves") signals the personal obligation of counting and is present in the Hebrew. LOGOS and PNEUMA both omit it.

**V. 15 — "Omer of the wave offering" / "sheaf of the wave offering"** [WORD]
- EMET: "the ***omer* of the wave offering** [*omer ha-tenufah*]"
- LOGOS: "the **sheaf** of the wave offering"
- PNEUMA: "the wave offering **of the sheaf**"
- **Ruling:** "the sheaf of the wave offering" — LOGOS. "The sheaf" is more accessible than the transliterated "*omer*" (EMET). PNEUMA reverses the construct relationship.

**V. 16 — "Week" / "Sabbath" for the seventh unit** [INTERP]
- EMET: "the day after the seventh **week**"
- LOGOS: "the day after the seventh **week**"
- PNEUMA: "the day after the seventh **Sabbath**"
- **Ruling:** "the seventh week" — EMET/LOGOS. *Ha-shabbat ha-shevi'it* can mean either "the seventh week" (since *shabbat* / *shabbatot* is used for "week" in counting contexts) or "the seventh Sabbath." LOGOS/EMET interpret as "week" in this count-context. PNEUMA reads "Sabbath," which is a defensible alternative but potentially misleading.

**V. 17 — "From your dwelling places" / "from wherever you live"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "from your **dwellings**"
- LOGOS: "from your **dwelling places**"
- PNEUMA: "**from wherever you live**"
- **Ruling:** "from your dwelling places" — LOGOS. See note at v. 3.

**V. 17 — "Baked with leaven" / "baked with yeast"** [WORD]
- EMET: "they shall be **baked leavened** [*chamets te'afenah*]"
- LOGOS: "they shall be **baked with leaven**"
- PNEUMA: "**baked with yeast**"
- **Ruling:** "baked with leaven" — LOGOS. "Leaven" (*chamets*) is the theologically weighted term used throughout the festival legislation; "yeast" (PNEUMA) is a modern equivalent that loses the connection to *chamets* as the term defining the Feast of Unleavened Bread.

**V. 18 — "One bull from the herd" / "one young bull"** [WORD]
- EMET: "one **bull of the herd** [*par echad ben-bakar*]"
- LOGOS: "one **bull from the herd**"
- PNEUMA: "one **young bull**"
- **Ruling:** "one bull from the herd" — LOGOS/EMET. PNEUMA's "young" renders *ben-bakar* ("son of the herd") interpretively; "from the herd" is more standard.

**V. 19 — "Peace offerings" / "fellowship offering"** [WORD]
- EMET: "a **peace offering** [*shelamim*]"
- LOGOS: "a sacrifice of **peace offerings**"
- PNEUMA: "a **fellowship offering**"
- **Ruling:** "a sacrifice of peace offerings" — LOGOS. "Fellowship offering" (PNEUMA, following NIV) is an interpretive rendering of *shelamim*; "peace offerings" is the established project term.

**V. 21 — "Proclaim on this very day" / "make a proclamation on the same day"** [WORD]
- EMET: "you shall proclaim on **this very day** [*be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh*]"
- LOGOS: "you shall make a **proclamation on the same day**"
- PNEUMA: "proclaim **a sacred assembly**"
- **Ruling:** "you shall proclaim on this very day" — EMET. The *etsem ha-yom* emphatic is preserved (see v. 14). LOGOS's "the same day" softens the emphasis. PNEUMA inserts "a sacred assembly" as the object of the verb — this is an addition beyond the Hebrew, which has no direct object after *u-qratem*.

**V. 22 — "Right to its edge" / "right up to its edge" / "all the way to the very edges"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "you shall not **complete the corner** of your field"
- LOGOS: "you shall not reap your field **right up to its edge**"
- PNEUMA: "do not reap **all the way to the very edges** of your field"
- **Ruling:** "you shall not reap your field right to its edge" — LOGOS adjusted. All three capture the *pe'ah* law; LOGOS's phrasing is cleanest. EMET's "complete the corner" is literal but requires inference about what "completing the corner" means. PNEUMA's "all the way to the very edges" is emphatic but redundant.

**V. 22 — "Sojourner" / "foreigner"** [WORD]
- EMET: "the **sojourner** [*ger*]"
- LOGOS: "the **sojourner**"
- PNEUMA: "the **foreigner**"
- **Ruling:** "sojourner" — project ruling for *ger*.

**V. 24 — "Solemn rest" / "rest" for *shabbaton*** [WORD]
- EMET: "a **rest** [*shabbaton*]"
- LOGOS: "a **solemn rest**"
- PNEUMA: "a day of **rest**"
- **Ruling:** "a solemn rest" — LOGOS. *Shabbaton* is a stronger term than plain *shabbat* and merits the intensifying "solemn." EMET's plain "rest" and PNEUMA's "day of rest" both underrender the term.

**V. 24 — "Memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" / "day of rest... signaled by trumpet blasts"** [STRUCT/WORD]
- EMET: "a **memorial of trumpet blast** [*zikron teru'ah*]"
- LOGOS: "a **memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast**"
- PNEUMA: "a sacred assembly **signaled by trumpet blasts**" (restructured)
- **Ruling:** "a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" — LOGOS. PNEUMA collapses *zikron teru'ah* into a modifier of "sacred assembly," losing the independent weight of *zikron* as its own noun. EMET's "memorial of trumpet blast" is accurately literal; LOGOS's "proclaimed by" adds a slight interpretive gloss but reads more naturally.

**V. 27 — "Day of Atonements" / "Day of Atonement"** [WORD/INTERP]
- EMET: "the Day of **Atonements** [*yom ha-kippurim* — plural]"
- LOGOS: "the Day of **Atonement**"
- PNEUMA: "the Day of **Atonement**"
- **Ruling:** "the Day of Atonements" — EMET. The Hebrew *kippurim* is grammatically plural. The English traditional "Day of Atonement" (singular) is an established convention, but "Day of Atonements" is more formally precise and reflects the comprehensive character of the day's purification. Applied consistently in vv. 27 and 28.

**V. 27 — "Afflict yourselves" / "humble yourselves"** [WORD/INTERP]
- EMET: "you shall **afflict yourselves** [*ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem*]"
- LOGOS: "you shall **afflict yourselves**"
- PNEUMA: "**humble yourselves**"
- **Ruling:** "you shall afflict yourselves" — EMET/LOGOS. This is the most theologically significant disagreement in the chapter. PNEUMA adopts the LXX rendering (*tapeinōsete tas psychas hymōn* — "you shall humble your souls"), which captures the internal disposition but loses the physical, bodily sense of the Hebrew *innui* (affliction, self-denial, fasting). The project terminology ruling explicitly mandates "afflict yourselves" for *ve-innitem*.
- *Applies at vv. 27, 29, and 32.*

**V. 28 — "All work" / "any work" / "no work"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "**all labor** [*kol-melakhah*] you shall not do"
- LOGOS: "you shall do **no work**"
- PNEUMA: "**Do no work** on that day"
- **Ruling:** "you shall do no work" — LOGOS. In v. 28 the MT uses *kol-melakhah* (the absolute Sabbath-level prohibition, not *melaket avodah*). All three agents effectively render this correctly; LOGOS's "no work" is cleanest.

**V. 29 — Pronoun for *ha-nefesh* (vv. 29–30)** [WORD/INTERP]
- EMET: "shall be cut off from **her** people [*me-ammeiha*]"
- LOGOS: "shall be cut off from **his** people"
- PNEUMA: "must be cut off from **their** people"
- **Ruling:** "her people" — EMET. The Hebrew *ha-nefesh* is a feminine noun; *me-ammeiha* and *mi-qerev ammah* (v. 30) use feminine suffixes. EMET preserves the Hebrew gender; LOGOS normalizes to masculine; PNEUMA uses a modern gender-inclusive plural pronoun. The consensus preserves the formally exact feminine pronoun of the Hebrew.

**V. 30 — "That person I will destroy" / "I will destroy... anyone who"** [STRUCT]
- EMET: "I will destroy **that person** [*ha-nefesh ha-hi*] from the midst of **her** people"
- LOGOS: "**that person** I will destroy from among **his** people"
- PNEUMA: "I will destroy from among **their** people **anyone** who does any work on that day"
- **Ruling:** "that person I will destroy from among her people" — EMET/LOGOS combined. LOGOS's fronted "that person" mirrors the MT's emphasis on *ha-nefesh ha-hi*. PNEUMA restructures the clause and loses the direct focus on the named person.

**V. 32 — "At evening" / "beginning at evening"** [WORD/ADD]
- EMET: "in the evening [*ba-erev*]"
- LOGOS: "**beginning at** evening"
- PNEUMA: "**From** the evening of"
- **Ruling:** "at evening" — EMET, adjusted. LOGOS's "beginning at" and PNEUMA's "From" add words not in the MT's plain *ba-erev*. The consensus renders "at evening."

**V. 34 — "Feast of Booths" / "Festival of Tabernacles"** [WORD]
- EMET: "the Feast of **Booths** [*chag ha-sukkot*]"
- LOGOS: "the Feast of **Booths**"
- PNEUMA: "the LORD's Festival of **Tabernacles**"
- **Ruling:** "the Feast of Booths" — EMET/LOGOS. "Booths" is the literal translation of *sukkot* (temporary shelters); "Tabernacles" (PNEUMA) is the traditional Latin-derived English term (from the KJV tradition) but is less transparent and may evoke the Tabernacle (*mishkan*) rather than the harvest shelters (*sukkot*).

**V. 36 — "Solemn assembly" / "closing assembly"** [WORD]
- EMET: "a **solemn assembly** [*atseret*]"
- LOGOS: "a **solemn assembly**"
- PNEUMA: "the **closing assembly**"
- **Ruling:** "a solemn assembly" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "closing assembly" accurately captures the etymology of *atseret* (from *atsar*, "to hold back/close") but is not the established English rendering. "Solemn assembly" (ESV/NASB/KJV) is standard.

**V. 37 — "Each on its proper day" / "each day's matter in its day"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "**each day's matter in its day** [*devar yom be-yomo*]"
- LOGOS: "each on **its proper day**"
- PNEUMA: "each on **its proper day**"
- **Ruling:** "each on its proper day" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's more literal "each day's matter in its day" preserves the Hebrew idiom *devar yom be-yomo* but is slightly wooden in English.

**V. 38 — "Besides" / "in addition to"** [WORD]
- EMET: "**apart from** the sabbaths of the LORD... **apart from** your gifts"
- LOGOS: "**besides** the LORD's sabbaths... **besides** your gifts"
- PNEUMA: "**in addition to** the LORD's Sabbaths... **and in addition to** your gifts"
- **Ruling:** "besides" — LOGOS. Both "besides" and "apart from" accurately render *mi-levad*. PNEUMA's "in addition to" is natural but loses the slightly contrastive sense of *mi-levad* ("over and above / distinct from"). EMET's "apart from" is close; LOGOS's "besides" is the more established English equivalent for the enumeration formula.

**V. 39 — "Celebrate the feast" / "keep the feast"** [WORD]
- EMET: "you shall **keep** the feast [*tachoggu*]"
- LOGOS: "you shall **celebrate** the feast"
- PNEUMA: "**celebrate** the festival of the LORD"
- **Ruling:** "you shall celebrate the feast" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The verb *chagag* means "to keep/celebrate a pilgrimage feast"; "celebrate" captures the joyful character of *chagag* better than "keep."

**V. 39 — "Produce of the land" / "crops of the land"** [WORD]
- EMET: "the **produce** [*tevu'at*] of the land"
- LOGOS: "the **produce** of the land"
- PNEUMA: "the **crops** of the land"
- **Ruling:** "the produce of the land" — LOGOS/EMET. *Tevu'at* refers to the yield/produce of the land; "produce" is more precise than "crops" in this context.

**V. 40 — "Fruit of splendid trees" / "branches from splendid trees"** [WORD/OMIT]
- EMET: "the **fruit** [*peri*] of a majestic tree"
- LOGOS: "the **fruit** of splendid trees"
- PNEUMA: "**branches** from splendid trees"
- **Ruling:** "the fruit of splendid trees" — LOGOS. This is an error in PNEUMA: *peri* means "fruit," not "branches." PNEUMA apparently conflated the first item (fruit) with the subsequent items (branches, boughs, willows) and rendered all of them as "branches." The Hebrew is unambiguous: *peri ets hadar* = "fruit of a splendid tree."

**V. 40 — "Majestic" / "splendid" for *hadar*** [WORD]
- EMET: "fruit of a **majestic** tree"
- LOGOS: "fruit of **splendid** trees"
- PNEUMA: "branches from **splendid** trees"
- **Ruling:** "splendid" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. Both "splendid" and "majestic" accurately render *hadar* (glory, splendor, majesty); "splendid" is the established ESV rendering.

**V. 40 — "Branches of palm trees" / "palm fronds"** [WORD]
- EMET: "**fronds** of palm trees [*kappot temarim*]"
- LOGOS: "**branches** of palm trees"
- PNEUMA: "**palm fronds**"
- **Ruling:** "branches of palm trees" — LOGOS. *Kappot* = "branches/fronds." LOGOS's full "branches of palm trees" parallels the subsequent clauses and is preferred.

**V. 40 — "Boughs of leafy trees" / "leafy boughs"** [WORD/OMIT]
- EMET: "**boughs of leafy trees** [*anaf ets avot*]"
- LOGOS: "**boughs of leafy trees**"
- PNEUMA: "**leafy boughs**"
- **Ruling:** "boughs of leafy trees" — LOGOS/EMET. PNEUMA's "leafy boughs" drops "of trees" (*ets avot* = "of dense/leafy trees"), compressing the formula.

**V. 42 — "Native Israelites" / "every native in Israel"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "every **native** [*ezrach*] **in Israel**"
- LOGOS: "All **native Israelites**"
- PNEUMA: "all **native-born Israelites**"
- **Ruling:** "all native Israelites" — LOGOS. EMET's "every native in Israel" is accurate but less idiomatic. PNEUMA's "native-born" adds "born" not in the Hebrew *ezrach*.

**V. 43 — "Made the sons of Israel dwell" / "made the Israelites live in temporary shelters"** [WORD]
- EMET: "I caused the sons of Israel to **dwell** in **booths**"
- LOGOS: "I made the sons of Israel **dwell** in **booths**"
- PNEUMA: "I made the Israelites **live** in **temporary shelters**"
- **Ruling:** "I made the sons of Israel dwell in booths" — LOGOS. PNEUMA uses "Israelites" (drops "sons of"), "live" (acceptable but less formal than "dwell"), and "temporary shelters" (an interpretive gloss on *sukkot* — the word means "booths/shelters/huts" and is the name of the festival itself; "temporary shelters" is explanatory, not translational).

**V. 44 — "Thus Moses declared" / "And Moses declared" / "So Moses announced"** [WORD/STRUCT]
- EMET: "**And** Moses **declared** [*va-yedabber*]"
- LOGOS: "**Thus** Moses **declared**"
- PNEUMA: "**So** Moses **announced**"
- **Ruling:** "Thus Moses declared" — LOGOS. "Declared" is more formal than PNEUMA's "announced" for *dibber* in this summary context. LOGOS's "Thus" signals the summary character of the verse. EMET's opening "And" is also defensible as a rendering of *va-yedabber*; "Thus" is preferred.

---

## Cross-Cutting Systemic Disagreements

**Systemic 1: "Fire-offering" vs. "food offering" for *isheh la-YHWH*** [WORD/INTERP]
EMET uses "fire-offering" at every occurrence; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "food offering." Applies at vv. 8, 13, 18, 25, 27, 36, and 37. Ruling: "food offering" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The lexical dispute over *isheh* is unresolved in scholarship; the consensus follows modern formal-equivalent convention.

**Systemic 2: *Kol-melakhah* vs. *melaket avodah*** [WORD/INTERP]
The most important structural disagreement in the chapter. LOGOS uses "ordinary work" and PNEUMA uses "regular work" for both the absolute *kol-melakhah* prohibition (Sabbath, v. 3; Day of Atonement, v. 28) and the lesser *melaket avodah* prohibition (festival days, vv. 7, 8, 21, 25, 35, 36), effectively flattening the distinction. EMET correctly distinguishes: "all labor [*kol-melakhah*]" for the Sabbath and "all laborious work [*melaket avodah*]" for festival days. The consensus preserves this distinction: "no work" (v. 3, v. 28) vs. "no laborious work" (vv. 7, 8, 21, 25, 35, 36).

**Systemic 3: "Holy convocations" vs. "sacred assemblies"** [WORD]
PNEUMA uses "sacred assemblies" throughout the chapter for *miqra'ei qodesh*. EMET and LOGOS use "holy convocations." Ruling: "holy convocations" at every occurrence. This is a recurring disagreement affecting vv. 2, 3, 4, 7, 8, 21, 24, 25, 27, 35, 36.

**Systemic 4: "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites"** [WORD]
PNEUMA uses "Israelites" throughout; EMET and LOGOS use "sons of Israel." Ruling: "sons of Israel." Applies at vv. 2, 10, 24, 34, 42, 43, 44.

**Systemic 5: "Afflict yourselves" vs. "humble yourselves"** [WORD/INTERP]
PNEUMA uses "humble yourselves" throughout for *ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem*; EMET and LOGOS use "afflict yourselves." Ruling: "afflict yourselves" per project terminology ruling. Applies at vv. 27, 29, and 32.

---

## Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Word choice [WORD] | 29 |
| Structural/clause [STRUCT] | 7 |

## Notable Observation

The most consequential disagreement in this chapter is **PNEUMA's substitution of "humble yourselves" for "afflict yourselves"** at the Day of Atonement passages (vv. 27, 29, 32). This follows the LXX (*tapeinōsete tas psychas hymōn* — "humble your souls") but loses the physical, embodied, bodily-denial sense of the Hebrew *inui nefesh*. Rabbinic halakha specifies five bodily deprivations (fasting, no bathing, no anointing, no leather sandals, no conjugal relations) derived precisely from the physical-affliction sense of the verb *anah*. "Humble yourselves" spiritualizes what the Hebrew intends as a somatic practice. This represents a genuine interpretive divergence, not merely a stylistic preference.

The second most consequential systemic disagreement is **LOGOS and PNEUMA's flattening of *kol-melakhah* and *melaket avodah*** into a single English phrase ("ordinary work" / "regular work"), which erases a deliberate legal distinction preserved throughout the Priestly legislation and visible in the parallel festival calendars of Numbers 28–29 and Deuteronomy 16.
